stories.title: Churchill seizing Pebb Capital’s Streeterville hotel eyed for apartments conversion in $28M foreclosure
stories.excerpt: Todd Rosenberg’s plan to convert a Streeterville hotel into apartments is ending in a $28 million foreclosure, capping a yearslong saga of financial distress and insider dealmaking for the property at 201 East Delaware Place. Rosenberg’s firm, Boca Raton, Florida-based Pebb Capital, last month agreed to surrender the 18-story building to lender Churchill Real Estate. That company has offices in New York and Charlotte, North Carolina, and has been active in Chicago’s distressed property debt market, according to Cook County Circuit Court records. The hotel, which is out of operation, was known as the Gale Chicago in recent years and […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pebb Capital's 18-story Streeterville hotel at 201 E Delaware foreclosed by Churchill Real Estate for $28M.
Planned residential conversion derailed; Chicago lodging market still depressed post-pandemic.
Distressed hotel-to-apartment pivots facing financing headwinds across Chicago Loop.
stories.summary: Pebb Capital's plan to convert the defunct Raffaello/Gale Chicago hotel (201 E Delaware) into apartments has ended in foreclosure to lender Churchill Real Estate. The $28M foreclosure reflects ongoing distress in Chicago's downtown hotel market, which has not fully recovered from the pandemic. Other developers, including Churchwick Partners and Blackstone entities, are attempting similar hotel-to-residential conversions but face complications from distressed debt.
stories.impact: Chicago's prolonged hotel recovery signals weak near-term demand for downtown conversion plays; developers pursuing speculative apartment conversions face tightened financing, pushing capital toward market-rate projects in stronger submarkets. Opportunity window for distressed acquisitions remains, but execution risk on value-add conversions is elevated.

stories.title: New York Area the Nation’s Most Promising for Film, TV Production: CBRE
stories.excerpt: At an uncertain time for television and film production nationwide as projects scatter across the globe and capitalize on massive tax benefits and other financial incentives, the New York tri-state area is the only production market in the U.S. that is clearly on the upswing, Commercial Observer has learned. According to a new report from CBRE, exciting metrics for the area’s production community show that spending on film and TV production increased 21 percent in 2025 over the previous year. Plus, soundstage space increased by 43 percent since 2020 (with more on the way), and leasing activity in Manhattan for film and TV production firms reached 755,000 square feet in 2025, the highest total since 2021. The report notes that in the wake of industry-shattering strikes by the writers and actors unions in 2023, the New York tri-state area is the only production market in the U.S. that has seen production starts recover to pre-2023 levels. “The region is emerging as a clear leader with a new wave of major studio investment and dedicated production partnerships that are reshaping the market,” Anthony Jasenski, CBRE Americas’ film production studio practice leader, said in a statement to CO. “This report underscores New York’s staying power even amid industry consolidation and shifting production strategies.” Drivers of this success include New York and New Jersey offering a combined $1.2 billion in tax incentives in 2026, as well as the increase in regional soundstage space recently introducing an influx of “modern, purpose-built facilities that allow the region to capture more stage-based, high-value production,” according to the report. Several major new studios have either opened in the area over the past year or so or are preparing to in the years to come. Sunset Pier 94 Studios in Manhattan, a collaboration between Vornado Realty Trust, Hudson Pacific Properties and Blackstone Real Estate, opened in January on the borough’s west side offering 232,000 square feet of
bullets: NY tri-state film/TV production spending up 21% YoY in 2025; soundstage space +43% since 2020.
Manhattan production firm leasing reached 755k SF in 2025, highest since 2021.
Only U.S. production market showing clear upswing post-2023 writers/actors strikes.
stories.summary: The New York tri-state area is the only major U.S. film and television production market experiencing robust growth, with production spending up 21% in 2025 and soundstage capacity expanding 43% since 2020. Manhattan office leasing for production firms hit 755,000 square feet in 2025, the highest level since 2021. CBRE notes the region is recovering faster than other production hubs nationwide following industry strikes.
stories.impact: Growth in production spending and office leasing in Manhattan signals emerging tenant demand for creative/media office space; creates near-term absorption for Class A office conversions and mixed-use projects targeting creative tenants in Chelsea, Midtown, and Long Island City. Developers should track additional soundstage and production space entitlements as a leading indicator of sustained employment and population draw to the tri-state.

stories.title: Capital One Provides $91M Letter of Credit for Far Rockaway Affordable Housing Project
stories.excerpt: Tishman Speyer has sealed $214 million of construction financing to develop a 100 percent affordable housing property in Far Rockaway, Queens, Commercial Observer has learned. Capital One closed a $91.4 million letter of credit to backstop a loan provided by the New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HDP) to support construction of the 300-unit Edgemere Commons B2 project. The bank is also purchasing low-income tax credits through a fund with hudson housing capital to fund the 17-story building spearheaded by Tishman Speyer’s affordable housing platform, TS Communities. “This partnership on Edgemere Commons B2 underscores our community benefits plan commitment to addressing the affordable housing crisis by investing in safe, stable and sustainable housing,” Loretta Duffy, senior director at Capital One, said in a statement. TS Communities closed $214 million of total development financing, which includes a debt package that features a combination of tax-exempt bonds and subsidy loans from HDP and the New York City Housing Development Corporation (HDC) through New York City’s New Construction Finance Program. The development, which will also feature 6,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, is slated for compilation in late 2028. Michelle Adams, senior managing director at Tishman Speyer, said in a statement that the financing marks “another important step forward in creating a vibrant, affordable neighborhood” in Far Rockaway. Located at 332 Beach 52nd Street, Edgemere Commons B2’s apartments will range from studios to three-bedroom layouts with 255 of the apartments set aside for households earning between 30 and 80 percent of area median income (AMI). The remaining 45 units will be designated for supportive housing to formerly homeless individuals that will be managed by Breaking Ground, a social service provider who also partnered with TS Communities for Building A2 in Edgemere Commons. Edgemere Commons B2 marks the fourth of 11 planned
bullets: Tishman Speyer closes $214M construction financing for 300-unit 100% affordable in Far Rockaway, Queens.
Capital One provides $91.4M letter of credit; includes LIHTC purchases via Hudson Housing Capital.
Edgemere Commons B2 (17-story) demonstrates strong capital availability for affordable multifamily in outer boroughs.
stories.summary: Tishman Speyer's TS Communities platform has closed $214 million in construction financing for Edgemere Commons B2, a 300-unit fully affordable housing project in Far Rockaway, Queens. Capital One issued a $91.4 million letter of credit backstopping a loan from NYC's Department of Housing Preservation and Development and is purchasing low-income housing tax credits. The project will deliver a 17-story building by tapping multiple subsidy layers.
stories.impact: Far Rockaway's absorption of 300 affordable units signals sustained investor appetite for LIHTC-backed deals in outer-borough submarkets; demonstrates capital flow toward workforce housing in transit-accessible locations. Developers should monitor NYC's HPD pipeline and LIHTC availability as constraints; strong institutional capital (Capital One, Tishman Speyer) signals competitive pressure for mission-driven projects in underserved geographies.

stories.title: Kidder Mathews Arranges Sale of 22-Unit Fremont Apartment Property
stories.excerpt: The Francis, a 22-unit apartment community located at 3636 Francis Avenue N. in Seattle’s Fremont neighborhood, has sold for $7 million. Matt Laird and Jack Shephard of Kidder Mathews’ Simon | Anderson Multifamily Team served as the exclusive brokers representing the seller, Trimark Property Group, and sourced the buyer, Curtis Capitol Group. Built in 1988, The Francis offers a mix of studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom units. The acquisition delivers Curtis Capitol Group a value-add opportunity featuring updated building systems and continued upside potential. “This transaction reflects the continued depth of investor demand for quality multifamily investments in the city’s urban core, assets that are difficult to replicate and located where renters consistently want to live,” said Laird. Situated on the north side of the Lake Washington Ship Canal, just miles from downtown Seattle, the property is surrounded by employers, dining, shopping, and cultural amenities. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Kidder Mathews Arranges Sale of 22-Unit Fremont Apartment Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Curtis Capitol Group acquires 22-unit Fremont apartment (The Francis, 1988-built) for $7M.
Value-add play in Seattle's urban core; $318k/unit pricing reflects tight supply of older multifamily.
Fremont location near downtown, employers, and amenities cited as replication difficulty.
stories.summary: The Francis, a 22-unit apartment building in Seattle's Fremont neighborhood, sold for $7 million to Curtis Capitol Group, representing a value-add acquisition of a 1988-built property. The $318k per-unit sale reflects strong investor demand for quality urban-core multifamily with embedded upside. Kidder Mathews brokers noted the property's scarcity and proximity to downtown, employers, and cultural amenities.
stories.impact: Seattle Fremont transaction signals continued investor appetite for older multifamily with renovation upside in walkable neighborhoods; limited supply of similar assets suggests rent-growth potential and development feasibility for new construction in Seattle's constrained core. No direct development signal, but validates stronghold demand for urban multifamily anchoring further value-add and new-build activity.

stories.title: ZSD bets on family-sized condos in West Loop
stories.excerpt: ZSD is planning a building that features family style condos, with four bedrooms minimum in each unit.  The development, dubbed 1282 Washington on West Washington Boulevard, has pre-sold 12 of the 16 total condos the building will allow for, according to Crain’s. The price per unit sold spanned $1.75 million to $3.2 million, with the remaining four up for a range of $2 million to $3 million. Every unit will have some outdoor space, according to the outlet. The building itself will span five stories. The site was previously owned by a toy company and spans 18,000 square feet.  One […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: ZSD pre-sold 12 of 16 luxury family condos in West Loop at $1.75M–$3.2M per unit.
1282 Washington: 5-story, four-bedroom-minimum condo play on school district proximity; completion fall 2027.
Site purchased April 2024 for $5M; reflects school-driven demand in West Loop family market.
stories.summary: Chicago developer ZSD has pre-sold 12 of 16 planned family-sized condos (four bedrooms minimum) at 1282 Washington in the West Loop, with units ranging from $1.75 million to $3.2 million. The five-story, 16-unit project emphasizes proximity to Mark T. Skinner Elementary West and local schools. ZSD acquired the 18,000-square-foot site for $5 million in April 2024 and expects completion by fall 2027.
stories.impact: Strong pre-sales (75% absorbed) in luxury family condo segment signals durable demand in West Loop driven by school quality; reflects parent demographic migration back to urban core with quality K–12 access. Validates opportunity for family-oriented, walkable urban multifamily and condo development anchored by school district reputation, particularly in Chicago's revitalizing neighborhoods.

stories.title: New Tucson Tribal Casino Reveals Start Date
stories.excerpt: Casino Del Sol, the resort enterprise owned and operated by the Pascua Yaqui Tribe, confirmed November 16 as the official opening date of Casino Del Sol, Vahi Taaʼam — the Tribe’s third gaming property. Located at 1055 W. Grant Road at Interstate 10, the 163,000-square-foot destination will include a 52,334-square-foot gaming floor that will feature 924 slot machines — including 56 dedicated High Limit games — seven bar-top gaming stations at the Center Bar, table games, a High Limit Lounge, and a SolSports sportsbook offering wagering on all major sports. Four food and beverage destinations will include the OP Grill (Old Pascua Grill), a 174-seat upscale casual restaurant drawing on distinctly local flavors; Sewa Market, bringing together UME, Streetscape, and Abuelitas Taqueria under one roof; and three bars — the Center Bar, the OP Grill Bar, and the High Limit Bar. A four-level parking structure with more than 1,200 spaces will serve guests on arrival. The post New Tucson Tribal Casino Reveals Start Date appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Pascua Yaqui Tribe opens Casino Del Sol Vahi Taaʼam in Tucson Nov 16; 163k SF with 924 slots, restaurants.
1,200-space parking structure; third gaming property for tribe; located at I-10 and W Grant Road.
No direct multifamily impact; hospitality/tourism demand driver for Tucson.
stories.summary: Casino Del Sol, owned and operated by the Pascua Yaqui Tribe, opens November 16 as the tribe's third gaming property in Tucson. The 163,000-square-foot property at I-10 and W Grant Road includes a 52,334-square-foot gaming floor, 924 slot machines, table games, restaurants, bars, and 1,200-space parking.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to multifamily development. Gaming and hospitality anchor may increase regional employment and visitor traffic, creating marginal incremental demand for hotel rooms and transient housing; potential modest boost to Tucson's service-sector employment but not a material supply or demand signal for apartment development in the near term.

stories.title: CU Denver Buys 25-Story Downtown Highrise
stories.excerpt: The University of Colorado Board of Regents okayed $29.75 million for the purchase of Independence Place, a 25-story downtown office tower. The purchase price includes the 55-year-old tower, two annexes and three levels of underground parking. The building, which is 79% vacant, was owned by an Alaskan public pension fund, which paid $144 million for it in 2007. The Denver Business Journal reports that after the meeting, the school said that extending further into downtown will enhance the student experience and position CU Denver as a workforce, economic and business development engine in the heart of the Central Business District. CU Denver operates in several downtown Denver buildings, most of them at the 150-acre Auraria Campus that sits adjacent to the city center. Funds for the purchase will come from CU Denver campus reserves. CU Denver expects to generate income through external leases, events and parking. The post CU Denver Buys 25-Story Downtown Highrise appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CU Denver purchases 25-story Independence Place downtown for $29.75M; building 79% vacant, built 1969.
Original buyer paid $144M in 2007; price drop reflects office-market distress in Denver CBD.
Institutional buyer repurposing office for campus use; no residential conversion signal.
stories.summary: CU Denver's Board of Regents approved a $29.75 million purchase of Independence Place, a 55-year-old, 25-story downtown office tower with 79% vacancy, owned by an Alaskan public pension fund that had paid $144 million in 2007. The transaction includes two annexes and three levels of underground parking. CU Denver plans to generate income through external leases, events, and parking.
stories.impact: Denver CBD office distress (79% vacancy, 80% decline in value over 18 years) reflects structural oversupply; limits new multifamily demand in downtown core. However, institutional repurposing and potential for mixed-use/residential conversion of distressed office assets in adjacent years could create pockets of opportunity. Developers should monitor other distressed office towers in Denver CBD for conversion feasibility.

stories.title: Hillwood Adding 1.2M SF of Warehouse Space to AllianceTexas
stories.excerpt: Hillwood plans to develop Alliance Westport 16, a 1,214,969-square-foot Class A speculative industrial building in the Alliance Logistics District. Construction has commenced, and completion is expected in July 2027. Hillwood’s projects in AllianceTexas currently include 8.2 million square feet under construction or in design, comprising 6.8 million square feet currently under construction and 1.4 million square feet in design. The district is anchored by BNSF’s Alliance Intermodal Facility and designed to support efficient freight movement across air, rail and highway networks. Alliance Westport 16 will benefit from Denton County’s low tax rate and several economic incentives, including the Triple Freeport Inventory Tax Exemption and Foreign Trade Zone status within Alliance FTZ #196. The project also has access to a Centralized Examination Station and U.S. Customs and Border Protection services. The project is designed by GSR Andrade Architects. Hillwood Construction Services will serve as the general contractor, and financing was done by Fifth Third Bank. The post Hillwood Adding 1.2M SF of Warehouse Space to AllianceTexas appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hillwood starts 1.21M SF Class A speculative industrial (Alliance Westport 16) at AllianceTexas; completion July 2027.
8.2M SF total pipeline in AllianceTexas (6.8M under construction, 1.4M in design).
Triple Freeport tax exemption and FTZ incentives; Fifth Third Bank financing; BNSF intermodal anchor.
stories.summary: Hillwood has commenced construction on Alliance Westport 16, a 1.21 million-square-foot Class A speculative industrial building in AllianceTexas, with completion expected July 2027. Hillwood's total AllianceTexas pipeline includes 8.2 million square feet, with 6.8 million currently under construction. The project benefits from Denton County tax incentives, Foreign Trade Zone status, and proximity to BNSF's intermodal facility.
stories.impact: Robust speculative industrial pipeline in Fort Worth logistics corridor signals confidence in e-commerce, distribution, and supply-chain demand; may drive regional employment and population influx to North Texas. Secondary effect: increased logistics/warehouse employment supports downstream demand for workforce housing and multifamily development in Denton County and northern DFW submarkets. Monitor employment data and wage growth in alliancetexas supply-chain jobs.

stories.title: McKesson Building $179M OKC Distribution Center
stories.excerpt: McKesson Corporation, a Fortune 100, U.S.-based company that helps ensure patients can access medicines, has selected Moore, Oklahoma , as the location for a $179 million automated regional distribution facility at the North Moore Industrial Park. The new facility will replace an existing distribution center, expanding capacity and modernizing capabilities to support future demand. McKesson distributes approximately one-third of all pharmaceuticals in North America and operates a digitally connected, automated distribution network designed to support the delivery of complex therapies to pharmacies and hospitals. The 330,000-square-foot facility will serve as a regional pharmaceutical distribution hub, combining digitally enabled logistics, state of the art automation, precision inventory management, and expanded cold chain capacity, helping ensure critical medicines reach customers safely and efficiently. Kadean Construction will be the construction partner for this distribution facility. Delivery is expected in 2028. The post McKesson Building $179M OKC Distribution Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: McKesson builds $179M, 330k SF automated pharmaceutical distribution hub in Moore, Oklahoma; delivery 2028.
Fortune 100 distributor (one-third of N.A. pharma) modernizing regional capacity at North Moore Industrial Park.
Job creation and logistics-sector employment growth signal for OKC metro.
stories.summary: McKesson Corporation, a Fortune 100 pharmaceutical distributor, is constructing a $179 million, 330,000-square-foot automated regional distribution facility in Moore, Oklahoma. The facility will replace an existing distribution center and expand capacity with digital logistics, automation, and cold-chain capabilities. Delivery is expected in 2028.
stories.impact: Major employer capex investment in Moore/OKC signals job creation in logistics, operations, and support roles; expect secondary demand for workforce housing in OKC metro, particularly in proximity to North Moore Industrial Park. Monitor OKC wage and employment growth to inform multifamily development activity in suburban metros; favorable for value-add and workforce-housing development in Oklahoma City and Moore submarkets over next 2–3 years.

stories.title: Blackstone Lands $115M Refi for Fort Lauderdale W Hotel From JP Morgan
stories.excerpt: Blackstone nabbed $115 million to refinance the oceanfront, W hotel in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., property records show. The debt from J.P. Morgan Chase covers the 346-room property at 401 North Fort Lauderdale Beach Boulevard, between Riomar Street and Bayshore Drive. In 2024, the private equity giant purchased the 19-story building from an affiliate of Related Companies. The $153 million deal included $97.6 million for the property’s real estate and another $55 million for the hotel’s operations and furniture. The 567,524-square-foot asset was built in 2009, and Related Companies spent about $55 million on renovations. The W hotel’s debt transaction marks the second notable refinancing J.P. Morgan Chase has issued to South Florida resorts lately. In April, the bank, alongside Citi Real Estate Funding, provided $600 million for the 1,000-room, oceanfront Diplomat Beach Resort in Hollywood, which Trinity Investments and Credit Suisse Asset Management had bought for $835 million in 2023. The recent refinancing also comes as the W property in South Beach is shutting down for renovations after the billionaire Reuben Brothers purchased the 407-room asset for more than $400 million in 2024. Earlier this week, the owners laid off over 100 workers. Representatives for Blackstone did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Blackstone refinances 346-room W Fort Lauderdale for $115M via JP Morgan; oceanfront at 401 N Fort Lauderdale Beach.
Blackstone acquired asset from Related Companies in 2024 for $153M (RE + ops/FF); 567k SF built 2009.
Second major South Florida resort refi by JPM in months (Diplomat $600M in April); hospitality financing active.
stories.summary: Blackstone secured a $115 million refinance from JP Morgan Chase for the oceanfront W Hotel in Fort Lauderdale (346 rooms, built 2009, 567,524 SF). Blackstone purchased the property from Related Companies in 2024 for $153 million. The refi represents the second major South Florida resort financing from JPM, following a $600 million credit for the Diplomat Beach Resort in April.
stories.impact: Active hospitality refinancing market in South Florida signals stabilizing lodging values and lender confidence in resort recovery post-pandemic. Indirect multifamily relevance: strong hospitality capital availability and pricing stability reduce conversion pressure on hotels, limiting near-term supply of distressed office/hotel assets for residential conversion. Developers should track hospitality cap rates and refinance activity as leading indicators of appetite for mixed-use resort-residences in Miami, Fort Lauderdale, and Palm Beach.

stories.title: Brooklyn developer scores financing on one-time David Werner conversion target
stories.excerpt: David Werner’s loss is Hershy Silberstein’s gain. The Blue Sky Builders honcho landed $102 million in construction financing for the office-to-residential conversion of 311 West 43rd Street in Hell’s Kitchen, the Commercial Observer reported. S3 Capital provided the debt for the redevelopment of the Press Building. Silberstein’s involvement in the project wasn’t previously disclosed. Instead, it appeared that one of New York City’s conversion kings, David Werner, would be spearheading the redevelopment. Werner was in contract to buy the 193,000-square-foot building for slightly more than $40 million, The Real Deal reported in April, about one-third of the $131 million that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blue Sky Builders (Hershy Silberstein) lands $102M financing from S3 Capital for office-to-residential conversion at 311 W 43rd St.
Press Building (193k SF, Hell's Kitchen): 160-unit rental + 40k SF retail; 467-m tax abatement (25% affordable).
David Werner deal fell through; Silberstein acquired at discount after WeWork default and lease rejection in bankruptcy.
stories.summary: Blue Sky Builders' Hershy Silberstein secured $102 million in construction financing from S3 Capital for the office-to-residential conversion of 311 West 43rd Street (Press Building) in Hell's Kitchen. The project plans 160 rental units and 40,000 square feet of commercial space, leveraging the 467-m tax abatement program (25% affordable). Silberstein acquired the 193,000-square-foot property after David Werner's acquisition fell through, following WeWork's 2023 bankruptcy abandonment of a major lease.
stories.impact: Hell's Kitchen conversion demonstrates access to financing for office-to-residential plays in Manhattan; S3 Capital's debut in this category suggests lender appetite expanding. WeWork's market contraction created distressed asset opportunities; monitor other coworking-heavy office buildings for conversion eligibility. 467-m tax abatement makes mixed-income rentals financially viable in Midtown West, signaling potential for further conversions. Track Manhattan office vacancy and WeWork lease defaults by submarket to identify conversion pipelines.

stories.title: Workplace Safety Platform Intenseye Takes 9K SF at 220 West 19th Street
stories.excerpt: Intenseye, an artificial intelligence-powered workplace safety platform, has signed a full-floor, 9,485-square-foot lease at Red Circle NY’s 220 West 19th Street in Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood, according to landlord broker Avison Young. The security firm’s deal on the entire fourth floor was one of two new leases totaling 13,264 square feet at the 12-story office building between Seventh and Eighth avenues. Game Changer Ventures, an investment firm that supports companies in the sports, media, gaming and entertainment industries, also signed a 3,779-square-foot lease on a portion of the building’s 11th floor. The asking rents and the lengths of each lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for office space in Chelsea was $84.71 per square foot in the first quarter, according to Newmark data. Avison Young’s Michael Gottlieb, Joel Wechsler, Patrick Steffens and Thomas Usher represented the landlord in both deals. Intenseye was represented by JLL’s Sebastian Infante and Jamie Katcher, while Game Changer Ventures was represented by CBRE’s Evan Fiddle and Dan Jaffe. CBRE and JLL did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s requests for comment. “220 West 19th Street offers a compelling combination of character, upgrades and location,” Gottlieb said in a statement. “The addition of Intenseye and Game Changer Ventures reflects strong interest from forward-thinking firms seeking boutique space in Chelsea.” 220 West 19th Street sits midblock between Seventh and Eighth avenues. Built in 1910, the building has tenants including e-payment platform Flowglad, real estate firm the Corcoran Group, digital advertising firm Celtra, and gym Crunch Fitness. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Intenseye (AI workplace safety) leases 9,485 SF at 220 W 19th St (Chelsea, Manhattan); full fourth-floor deal.
Game Changer Ventures also signed 3,779 SF on 11th floor; total new leasing 13,264 SF at same building.
Average Chelsea asking rent $84.71/SF in Q1; small-floor deal signals creative/tech tenant activity in Chelsea.
stories.summary: Intenseye, an AI-powered workplace safety platform, signed a full-floor lease for 9,485 square feet at 220 West 19th Street in Chelsea, Manhattan. Game Changer Ventures (sports/media/gaming investment firm) also leased 3,779 square feet on the same building's 11th floor. The two deals total 13,264 square feet; average asking rent in Chelsea was $84.71 per square foot in Q1.
stories.impact: Small-floor leasing activity at $84.71/SF in Chelsea reflects modest tenant demand in creative/tech sectors but does not signal major employment influx or demand acceleration. Limited relevance to multifamily; minor confirmation that Manhattan office market has pockets of activity in tech/media but lacks catalytic momentum for speculative multifamily development. Monitor Chelsea office leasing velocity and tenant cohort (tech vs. creative) for signals of secondary demand for workforce housing.

stories.title: New York City’s Pied-a-Terre Tax Hasn’t Affected the Luxury Market — So Far
stories.excerpt: Pied-a-terre tax scare? Au contraire! For now at least. On May 26, the New York State Legislature enacted a tax targeting the wealthiest homeowners in New York City, a group that Mayor Zohran Mamdani and Gov. Kathy Hochul feel haven’t been paying their fair share. In an effort to get wealthier Gothamites pony up so the city can fund things like free child care and cleaner streets, an amendment to Article 30-C of the New York State Tax Law was added to tax city homes not listed in tax filings as primary residences. There are two phases to this tax law, which sunsets on June 30, 2031, and applies to owners of one- to three-family residential properties, stockholders in co-operative buildings, and condominium unit owners. Phase one, which runs July 1, 2026, to June 30, 2028, levies rates between 4 percent and 6.5 percent on properties with an assessed value of $1 million or more. Phase two, which runs from July 1, 2028, to June 30, 2031, means a yearly fee on a second (or third, or fourth, etc.) home worth over $5 million based on the price. The tax, after adjusting for the disconnect between assessed and market values for co-ops and condos, is expected to raise $500 million for the city, according to an analysis by New York City Comptroller Mark Levine. And while the city may — or may not, depending on whom you ask — benefit from the pied-a-terre tax, the overall consensus among the real estate industry and adjacent fields is that targeting the city’s big spenders isn’t a good plan, and has the potential to drive them away. It “may ultimately prove to be one of the most disruptive pieces of real estate legislation New York state has passed in years,” Bob Knakal, chairman and CEO of investment sales firm BK Real Estate Advisors, wrote in Commercial Observer in early June. “We can have a debate on whether or not this will deter purchases in New York — every state has separate tax structures for residents versus nonresidents,” said Alexander Goldfarb, managing director a
bullets: NYC pied-à-terre tax enacted May 26; phases in 4–6.5% tax (2026–2028), then yearly fee on homes >$5M (2028–2031).
Tax applies to non-primary residences (1–3 family, co-ops, condos); expires June 30, 2031.
Luxury market shows no measurable impact to date; potential chilling effect on foreign/ultra-HNW buyers TBD.
stories.summary: New York State enacted a pied-à-terre tax on May 26, targeting owners of non-primary residences valued above $1 million (4–6.5% in Phase 1, 2026–2028) and $5 million-plus properties (annual fee in Phase 2, 2028–2031). The tax applies to one- to three-family homes, co-op shares, and condo units, sunsetting June 30, 2031. To date, the luxury condo market shows no apparent dampening.
stories.impact: Tax implementation delayed until July 2026; too early to assess impact on luxury condo demand or pricing. Developers should model potential reduction in ultra-high-net-worth buyers' willingness to acquire second homes in NYC, which could soften demand for luxury rentals and condos if enforcement is strict. Secondary effect: reduced demand for high-end owner-occupied units may shift capital toward rental multifamily, potentially benefiting institutional apartment developers. Monitor luxury market absorption and pricing post-July 2026 implementation.

stories.title: Related Group, BH score approval for Rosewood Residences West Palm Beach
stories.excerpt: Related Group and BH Group’s planned Rosewood Residences in West Palm Beach got the go-ahead. The city of West Palm Beach approved the luxury condo tower at 2001 North Flagler Drive, a BH spokesperson confirmed. The property is next to the site BH and Terra are buying from billionaire Jeff Greene for $100 million, planning a pair of 31-story condo towers. The developers paid $34 million for the 0.8-acre site in 2024. Earlier this year, they filed plans for a 27-story, 90-unit luxury high-rise with more than 13,000 square feet of indoor amenities, a swimming pool on the fifth floor […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Group and BH Group win approval for Rosewood Residences West Palm Beach; 27-story, 90-unit luxury condos.
Site purchased 2024 for $34M (0.8 acres); ~3,400 SF average units; replaces 2001 medical office building.
Adjacent to $100M Jeff Greene land deal (Terra/BH); West Palm condo pipeline >2,000 units under development.
stories.summary: Related Group and BH Group received West Palm Beach city approval for Rosewood Residences at 2001 North Flagler Drive—a 27-story, 90-unit luxury residential tower with ~3,400 SF average units and 13,000+ SF of amenities. The developers paid $34 million for the 0.8-acre site in 2024. The project replaces a 2001 medical office building and complements their adjacent Ritz-Carlton Residences project (138 units, $200M construction loan from Madison Realty in March).
stories.impact: West Palm Beach condo pipeline exceeds 2,000 units; Related and BH are major players expanding luxury residential inventory. Sustained approval and financing activity (Ritz-Carlton $200M in March) signal robust capital availability for luxury condos in West Palm. Risk: pipeline oversaturation and potential softening in ultra-luxury pricing if market absorbs 2,000+ units rapidly. Developers should monitor absorption rates and pricing in West Palm luxury condo market; opportunity window may be closing for new entrants unless targeting workforce/missing-middle segments.

stories.title: Miami Design District brings another marquee mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Another mixed-use project designed by a globally recognized architecture firm is coming to the Miami Design District. Miami Design District Associates and Raycliff Capital plan to build Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot office-and-retail project that will include more than 92,000 square feet of office space and 22,500 square feet of retail, according to a press release. Construction is expected to kick off in August and wrap up in early 2028. The developer, a joint venture between Craig Robins’ Dacra, L Catterton Real Estate and Brookfield Properties, tapped global architecture firm Snøhetta to design the development which will rise on a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Sweetbird North approved in Miami Design District: 8-story, 120.5k SF mixed-use (92k office, 22.5k retail); construction Aug 2027.
Designed by Snøhetta; developers include Dacra (Craig Robins), L Catterton Real Estate, Brookfield Properties.
Office targeting creative industries; 17k SF site; completion early 2028. Also developing Miami Design Residences condo by Fouquet's.
stories.summary: Miami Design District Associates (Dacra, L Catterton RE, Brookfield) and Raycliff Capital plan Sweetbird North, an eight-story, 120,500-square-foot office-and-retail project with 92,000 SF of creative-industry office and 22,500 SF of retail. Designed by Snøhetta, construction begins August 2027 and wraps early 2028. The development team is also building Miami Design Residences by Fouquet's, a luxury condo project with architect David Chipperfield.
stories.impact: Continued momentum in Miami Design District mixed-use development signals sustained investor confidence in creative-office and luxury-residential demand in the submarket. Design District's appeal to international capital and branded residential (Fouquet's, Rosewood) validates walkable, amenity-rich urban submarkets. Secondary effect: office-to-residential pipelines and creative-industry office absorption support broader Miami's tech and creative-sector employment narrative. Developers should track Design District's ability to lease 92k SF of creative office as a forward indicator of Miami's tech-talent draw and downstream multifamily demand in Wynwood, Edgewater, and nearby residential.

stories.title: Will Miami commissioners put Live Local Act in legal crosshairs?
stories.excerpt: A Miami official wants the city to take a swing at Florida’s Live Local Act, which would put it in the ring alongside other localities that are punching back at the developer-friendly law. City Commissioner Damian Pardo sponsored a measure to instruct the city attorney to find ways to challenge the law’s validity and authorize him to pursue legal action, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Florida’s Live Local Act, signed by Gov. Ron DeSantis in 2023, offers tax breaks and zoning incentives to developers that build projects with below market-rate housing. It’s a controversial law that has supporters celebrating […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Commissioner Damian Pardo sponsoring measure to instruct city attorney to challenge Florida Live Local Act validity.
Live Local Act (DeSantis 2023): tax breaks + zoning for developers building below-market-rate housing.
Multiple jurisdictions suing; Hollywood won March lawsuit; Hillsborough County sued in March; >200 Live Local projects statewide.
stories.summary: Miami City Commissioner Damian Pardo has sponsored a measure instructing the city attorney to challenge the validity of Florida's Live Local Act (signed by Governor Ron DeSantis in 2023), which offers tax breaks and zoning relief to developers who include below-market-rate housing. Miami joins other municipalities in legal challenges; Hollywood won a lawsuit against a Live Local developer in March, and Hillsborough County sued the state the same month. More than 200 Live Local projects are in development statewide.
stories.impact: Legal challenges to Live Local Act create uncertainty for developers and lenders underwriting projects under the law; if Miami's challenge succeeds (and contagion spreads), tax incentives and zoning variances could be clawed back mid-construction, materially impacting project economics. Conversely, successful defense of the law (or local defeats) emboldens developers to pursue more Live Local deals. Developers should closely monitor litigation outcomes in Hollywood, Hillsborough, and Sarasota; legal risk on Live Local deals is rising, and pro-forma assumptions may require stress-testing. If law is weakened, expect slowdown in below-market-rate infill projects in South Florida unless developers can underwrite without subsidies.
